text: The surgeon plans the placement of surgical instruments for a laparoscopic Heller's myotomy, inserting a Veress needle at the inferior crease of the umbilicus. A telescope is positioned approximately five centimeters above the umbilicus, supra-umbilical. A Nathanson liver retractor is planned for placement in the epigastric region, with additional ports in the left and right hypochondrium.

The Nathanson liver retractor is introduced through the epigastric port and secured to the operating table stand, effectively retracting the left lobe of the liver upwards. The insufflation process begins with the irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests, followed by the attachment of tubing to start insufflation at a preset pressure of fifteen, with the flow rate initially set to one liter per minute, increasing to three liters per minute.

The Nathanson liver retractor is applied, and the laparoscopic telescope is inserted. The surgeon identifies the caudate lobe and pars flaccida, then opens the peritoneum of the pars flaccida to begin anterior esophageal mobilization from right to left.

The surgeon stretches the peritoneum to facilitate dissection, revealing the blue-colored vena cava beneath the caudate lobe. Harmonic and ligature instruments are used to open the peritoneum over the anterior part of the oesophagus.
The surgeon continues the anterior mobilization of the esophagus, using careful dissection techniques while monitoring the patient's vital signs, including ETCO2, ECG, respiratory rate, and pulse.

The surgeon performs anterior mobilization of the esophagus, using the left hand to stretch the peritoneum and the right hand to push the esophagus downward. The procedure involves entering the posterior mediastinum and achieving a good areolar plane for blunt mobilization. Approximately three to four centimeters of the esophagus have been mobilized, with care taken to avoid injury and prevent opening the left mediastinal pleura.

The surgeon performs posterior mobilization of the esophagus, creating a retro-esophageal window to place a sling for further manipulation. Care is taken to avoid excessive traction that could puncture the esophagus.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the left side of the esophagus from the left crus, using a grasper to create a window through the posterior aspect of the esophagus. An infant feeding tube is introduced and tied to allow the assistant to pull the esophagus through the tube, ensuring the diaphragm is not pierced during the procedure.

The surgeon pulls down the mobilized esophagus and places a sponge nearby in preparation for potential bleeding. The myotomy begins with the use of two graspers or a Maryland dissector to strip the anterior muscle layers. Care is taken to avoid excessive traction on the NG tube to prevent stretching and perforation. If bleeding occurs, the sponge is used to apply pressure. A harmonic scalpel may be used, ensuring the Teflon jaw faces the mucosa. The mucosa is visible as it pops out, and the procedure continues along the esophagus for four to six centimeters.

The surgeon carefully cuts and separates the muscle over the stomach, ensuring the Teflon jaw is oriented towards the mucosa. Minimal use of energy is employed as the mediastinal part of the esophagus is completed. The bulging mucosa is visible, and residual muscle fibers are addressed.

The surgeon uses a ligature to cut the fat around the esophagus, which lacks serosa. The serosa of the muscle with the fat is removed, revealing further bulging. A hook is used to cut on the stomach part. An interseed is placed to cover the esophageal mucosa to manage any oozing. The procedure progresses to a door fundoplication, involving the stomach and the left crus. The left margin of the esophageal muscle is carefully handled, and a surgeon's knot is tied using ethyl bond 2-0 or Dacron suture.

The surgeon ties a second intracorporeal surgeon's knot on the left split muscle of the esophagus, ensuring the mucosa is not pricked. The knot is tied using a double wrap followed by two opposite alternating wraps. The procedure continues with the third knot, involving the stomach and the left side of the esophageal muscle, contributing to gastropexy and preventing gastroesophageal reflux.

The surgeon mobilizes the short gastric vessels near the stomach fundus, ensuring at least eight to ten centimeters of the fundus is free for an anterior 180-degree wrap during the Dor fundoplication. The spleen is visible, and the surgeon navigates potential adhesions. A bite is taken on the stomach, and an interseed is placed between the stomach and the right muscle of the oesophagus. Care is taken not to take a deep bite on the right crus to avoid pricking the vena cava.

The surgeon ties a surgeon's knot using a double wrap followed by two opposite alternating wraps, with assistance to position the stomach over the esophagus for tension-free suturing. The apex of the right crus is visible as the knot is secured.

The surgeon sutures the esophageal muscle and the crura using an interseed for reinforcement. The needle is held by the right hand to take the wrap due to the small suture size, ensuring careful handling to avoid accidental pricks.

During the Heller's myotomy, the surgeon focuses on suturing and knotting techniques while mobilizing the esophagus and stripping tissue. A needle is removed, and an additional suture is placed to cover a small defect, ensuring complete coverage. The door fundoplication supports the mucosa and controls any small impending perforations with stomach pressure. The final knot is tied.
The surgeon identifies a small five-millimeter defect at the apex of the diaphragm. A suture is placed to close this defect, with the final knot securing the area.

The surgeon performs an appendectomy using a ligature introduced through the left hypochondriac port, which is parallel to the telescope. The Mishra's knot is tied at the base of the appendix, and despite the parallel alignment of the right hypochondriac and umbilical ports, the appendix is successfully removed through the umbilical cannula.
